$MELI is quietly entering the food delivery market. They are starting in Argentina. The early results are already very strong. The company views this as a smart bet that perfectly matches their core strengths. Their massive shipping network and daily user traffic give them a unique edge in this space. They are fully committed to this expansion. The team sees huge potential to grow this service and build a different kind of experience for their customers.

A quote from Ariel back in an interview a few months ago:

"I believe it is a strategic bet. We love to attach or add ventures to our core business where we feel we can add value. This is one where there is a strong alignment between our existing capabilities and the skills required to be successful in that industry.

We believe that the frequency of use and the power of our platform, combined with our logistics capabilities and other assets, give us a position where we can offer something unique and better serve the user. We are starting in Argentina; honestly, it is performing very well so far. We will see how it evolves, but we are quite committed. We believe there is significant synergy with Mercado Libre and that the potential is very high."

$MELI is quietly executing one of the most dominant growth strategies in global tech by combining a sprawling ecom marketplace with a highly profitable fintech ecosystem. In a recent interview, Mercado Pago's Head of Growth, Tomás Piuzzi, revealed the core drivers propelling the company forward.

First is the massive untapped market in Brazil and Mexico. While Mercado Pago has heavily penetrated Argentina with 22 to 24 million users, it has penetrated Brazil a lot less with a much larger population of 210 million. This leaves an enormous runway for continued user acquisition in the region's largest economies.

To capture this market, Mercado Pago is shifting its brand from a payment gateway to a full digital bank. The growth team is intentionally moving away from vanity metrics like app downloads and focusing entirely on high-value product adoption, specifically credit cards and interest-bearing accounts. This strategy drastically increases the average revenue per user. The credit portfolio is the engine of this growth. Piuzzi noted that merchants utilizing Mercado Credito grow their sales by 30% to 40% more than those who do not. Recent financial disclosures from Business Wire confirm this success, reporting that by Q4 2025, MELI’s credit portfolio surged 90% YoY to $12.5 B, while maintaining a historically low credit card 15-90-day NPL rate of 4.4%.

This creates a powerful, self-reinforcing ecosystem modeled after Asian super apps like WeChat. The ecom platform, which drove $19.9 billion in GMV in Q4, acts as a frictionless, low CAC funnel for the fintech side. 

The company’s organizational agility also serves as a massive competitive moat. Despite scaling to over 100,000 employees, MercadoLibre operates with startup velocity. They have successfully maintained over 30% revenue growth for 28 consecutive quarters, most recently posting 45% YoY revenue growth in Q4.

$MELI is choosing long-term dominance over short-term profits. Wall Street firms like J.P. Morgan have recently downgraded the stock over shrinking profit margins. The company is spending heavily to lower free shipping thresholds and expand credit to defend its Brazilian market share against aggressive rivals like $SE's Shopee. This margin dip is a calculated price to build a highly durable business. True compounding-style investing requires looking past temporary earnings compression and focusing on companies actively widening their economic moat.

MercadoLibre’s real advantage is a massive strategic shift from capturing wallet share to dominating time share. To combat Asian ecom giants, the company is ripping out its traditional horizontal product carousels and replacing them with an infinite vertical scroll. This change keeps users continuously entertained, generating a massive spike in unplanned purchases. They are successfully making the shopping experience feel much more like a social media feed than a static catalog.

The tech team is also obsessing over consumer psychology and micro-frictions. Instead of offering cash for product reviews, they tested a gamified system that rewarded users with digital stickers. This tweak multiplied user-uploaded photo reviews by 3.5x. They also relentlessly target the smallest UX details. For example, just changing a mobile login text field so that the @ symbol automatically appears on a user's keyboard boosted successful logins by 10%, translating to massive downstream sales volume.

Behind the scenes, MELI is fundamentally changing how it builds these products. As recently revealed by their VP of Technology, the company uses advanced AI as synthetic focus groups. Product managers feed specifications into LLM's to simulate confused users. The AI instantly flags logic flaws and misunderstandings, allowing the team to iterate on designs immediately before writing code. Human developers are now transitioning into context engineers who write detailed prompts for AI to execute.

This level of operational obsession is what separates a short-term trade from a long-term winner. While the broader market panics over quarterly logistics costs and shipping subsidies, MercadoLibre is quietly executing a behavioral economics, rapid experimentation, and AI integration.

TikTok Shop officially launched its direct sales channel in Brazil in May of last year. They saw explosive early adoption. It reached a GMV of $1 M per day just a few months after launching. $SE's Shopee is already an established giant in the region. Shopee currently holds a ~15% market share in Brazil. Shopee generated over R$70B in GMV last year. $MELI still dominates the landscape with an ~39% GMV.

In 2026 the landscape itself is defined by extreme logistical challenges, heavy tax regulations, and a shift toward unified ecosystems. Ecom in the region is moving from online retail to deep structural consolidation. Companies must integrate commerce, finance, and logistics to sruvive. Pure play retailers face unsustainable customer acquisition costs if they cannot offer financial yields, credit, or ad networks. And the new tax rules on cross-border goods have neutralized the old advantages of cheap foreign imports. This forces platforms to build local density. MercadoLibre leads this space by covering roughly 90% of its volume with its own fulfillment and cross-docking network.

Shopee’s strategy historically focused heavily on the low AOV segment. It uses aggressive pricing, frequent promotions, and a gamified mobile experience. To defend against taxes and speed up deliveries, Shopee operates 16 distribution centers in Brazil. TikTok Shop uses a completely different strategy with social commerce. TikTok Shop turns the marketing funnel upside down because discovery and checkout happen at the exact same time. Users do not search for products. The algorithm feeds them video content and live streams, creating instant impulse purchases.

The future of this battle comes down to logistics depth versus attention capture. Shopee must continue building local warehouses to protect its mass-market base from MercadoLibre’s aggressive free shipping cuts. TikTok Shop will force traditional platforms to integrate social features to keep users engaged. Ultimately, the winner will not just offer the lowest prices. The winner will be the platform that successfully merges viral social discovery with flawless local delivery and banking.

$AMZN is playing a massive game of attrition in Mexico, but $MELI holds a structural edge. Amazon’s strategy relies heavily on its global scale and cash flow. It uses profits from its high margin global operations to cross-subsidize its Mexican e-commerce operations. This allows Amazon to endure steep losses while heavily subsidizing fulfillment and delivery to capture market share. Amazon takes an asset-light approach to finance in Mexico. Because the country is highly underbanked, Amazon partners with third parties like INVEX Banco for digital accounts and Kueski Pay for BNPL credit. Amazon avoids taking credit risk on its own balance sheet, but this leaves its ecosystem fragmented and reliant on outside vendors.

MercadoLibre has a massive edge because it owns the entire ecosystem. MELI built its own logistics network, Mercado Envios, which handles over 75% of its shipments in Mexico and offers the fastest delivery in the region. More importantly, MELI owns Pago, its native fintech platform. Instead of outsourcing, MELI plans to operate as a full digital bank with its full banking licensing pending approval this year. This will allow it to seamlessly capture retail deposits, offer yielding accounts, as it continues issuing its own credit cards. By controlling the financial infrastructure, MELI gathers deep consumer data. This data allows MELI to underwrite loans with lower risk and acquire customers at a fraction of the cost of traditional banks. The seamless loop between shopping, shipping, and banking creates a structural moat that Amazon cannot easily replicate.

MercadoLibre views Mexico as its biggest opportunity because of the massive gap between digital adoption and financial inclusion. While smartphone usage is incredibly high, cash still dominates the economy. Only about ~20% of the Mexican population has a credit card, and roughly half remain unbanked. Ecom in Mexico is exploding, projected to reach $184 B by 2027 with a ~20%+ annual growth rate. MELI sees a 10X opportunity to bring millions of unbanked Mexicans into the formal financial system over the next five to seven years. To capture this, MELI committed billions in Mexico to expand logistics and AI. Mexico is huge blank canvas for MELI to prove its model can entirely replace traditional banking while dominating retail.

$MELI's platform is not avoiding social commerce. MercadoLibre introduced short-form video Clips in 2023 and also provides broadcast channels. Sellers are now building proprietary audiences directly within the app. Once a seller gains followers, they can send push notifications and exclusive discount coupons directly to those users. This creates a CAC of essentially zero for repeat buyers and builds a massive competitive moat.

Local logistics are beating centralized warehouses. Wall Street closely tracks MercadoLibre's full centralized fulfillment network. But on the ground in dense urban centers, buyers want instant gratification. Independent sellers using Mercado Envios Flex to deliver items on the exact same day via local couriers are capturing share and out-competing the centralized warehouse infrastructure.

Sellers are using combos to protect margins and dominate ads. Cheap imported goods are causing a price war on the platform. To survive, top sellers are bundling items to artificially raise their AOV. Izts difficult to run Mercado Ads on a low-ticket item because the cost-per-click destroys the margin. By bundling products into kits, sellers increase their margins enough to out-spend competitors on advertising and dominate search rankings.

The latest financial data proves the aggressive growth strategy is working. The market worried about margin pressure when MercadoLibre lowered its free shipping threshold to R$19 in Brazil. But Q4 earnings show this was a highly successful move. The lower threshold drove massive volume, leading to a 45% YoY revenue surge. At the same time, the Mercado Pago credit portfolio grew by 90% YoY to $12.5 B, which means the sticky ecosystem is growing in lock-step.

MercadoLibre is rapidly evolving into a high-margin advertising and credit powerhouse. The core e-commerce marketplace is shifting to a top-of-funnel mechanism that feeds these highly profitable engines.

Mercado Libre stock is down 32% from its peak, despite reaccelerating revenue growth of 45% last quarter. Investors are skeptical due to contracting margins over the past two years, fearing the company was overearning and is now facing a new paradigm of intense competition from Shopee at the low end and Amazon at the high end.  

Short-Term Catalysts/Factors:  Fintech Growth and Lending: The company is aggressively expanding its loan book, particularly to subprime borrowers in Brazil and Mexico. While this drives growth, it introduces significant credit risk that investors are watching closely.  

Competitive Defenses (Logistics vs. Price): To counter Shopee, Mercado Libre reduced free shipping thresholds from 79 Reais to 19 Reais, which squashed margins but helped defend their market position. They are also relying on their established logistics network to maintain fast shipping superiority over competitors.  

Fintech as a Moat: Mercado Pago's ability to offer high interest on deposits (15%) and provide credit to underbanked users acts as a major ecosystem hook, potentially becoming a larger profit pool than e-commerce in the future.

$MELI DCF valuation model - updated

4. WACC @ 10.9%

6. Tax rate 27% 
7. The input that drives reinvestment is the linearly regressed Sales to Capital ratio, from 1.9 recently to 1.3 in 2035

Historic Growth (for context) 
MercadoLibre has delivered exceptional historical growth, evolving from a regional marketplace into Latin America’s dominant e-commerce and fintech ecosystem. Over the past decade, revenue has compounded at well over 25% annually, driven by rapid expansion in Brazil, Mexico, and Argentina, surging logistics capabilities, and the explosive adoption of MercadoPago. Gross merchandise volume, unique buyers, and fintech TPV have all scaled dramatically, while EBITDA has expanded from modest levels to billions as the company gained operating leverage. This combination of platform effects, fintech penetration, and sustained demand for digital commerce has made MELI one of the fastest-growing large-cap companies globally - setting the backdrop for any forward-looking valuation.

Why I believe it can work out well
• Sustained high revenue growth
• Fintech flywheel gaining momentum
• Logistics moat is strengthening
• Operating leverage over time
• Large TAM still underpenetrated
• Ecosystem effects reinforce growth
• Strong balance sheet and disciplined execution
• Optionality from new verticals

What can go wrong
• Margin pressure persists
• Credit risk in fintech
• Currency volatility
• Intensifying competition
• Regulatory risk
• Macro instability
• Execution missteps

Conclusion
My intrinsic value for MercadoLibre is 1,973$. Valuation suggests that the stock is trading at 15% discount to fair value. If adjusted to FV within 3 years, it will generate +6% in annual alpha.

📰🧵🇦🇷 ALL OF THIS HAPPENED IN @JMilei's ARGENTINA THIS WEEK! Recorded from Greenwich, CT. #ARGENTINAWEEK

1️⃣ ARGENTINA WEEK WRAPS IN NEW YORK — GOVERNMENT TALLIES US$16B IN INVESTMENT ANNOUNCEMENTS
Argentina Week concluded Thursday after three days of panels, bilateral meetings, and investor pitches across JPMorgan, Bank of America, and the Argentine consulate in New York. The government tallied US$16.15 billion in announced investments.
📊 New announcements made during the event: 
• MercadoLibre ($MELI): US$3.4B for logistics expansion in Argentina 
• $TGS: US$3.0B for NGL industrialization infrastructure
📊 Expanded commitments from previously announced projects: 
• Pampa Energía $PAM: US$4.5B RIGI application for Rincón de Aranda in Vaca Muerta — an expansion of the original US$1.5B development plan following the government's decision to extend RIGI benefits to upstream oil and gas
• First Quantum Minerals (Canada): US$5.25B for the Taca Taca copper project in Salta — disclosed in a technical report last month, formalizing the Canadian miner's commitment to what the company called its "highest-probability near-term development project"
📈 The turnout: Ambassador @alejandrito reported 410 investors, 210 companies, 128 CEOs, 42 founders, 26 board chairs, and 11 Argentine governors. The consulate cocktail was planned for 150 people; 400 attended with 500 more on the waitlist.
💬 The investor mood: In conversations throughout the week, several international investors signaled that larger financial flows may await longer-term political signals — including the possibility of a Milei reelection in 2027 — and a sustained improvement in net reserves.

2️⃣ JAMIE DIMON OPENS WITH PRAISE FOR MILEI — "I HOPE THIS BECOMES AN EXAMPLE OF HOW TO FIX A COUNTRY"
JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon introduced @JMilei on Day 2 of Argentina Week, calling the turnaround "astonishing" and "something none of us would have predicted four or five years ago."
Dimon: "This president has very strong convictions about how to fix a country." He cited the drop in inflation from 200%+ to 2% monthly as "a miracle," and noted that Argentina is no longer monetizing debt, is attracting foreign investment, and is growing reserves.
"I'd love to see this become an example for the world — policies that work, policies that help all citizens, real reforms. And I think it's a great example for Latin America."
Milei used the platform to reiterate his moral framing: "Our decisions are sustained by what is just." He doubled down on attacks against Paolo Rocca (Techint) and Javier Madanes Quintanilla (Aluar/Fate), calling them "crony-capitalist businessmen" and saying "the corrupt Argentina is over."
On FX availability: Milei told BCRA chief @Kicker0024 — seated in the audience — "Get ready, Santiago, the dollars are going to come out of your ears. But be careful how you buy them — don't let them turn into inflation."
⚡ The consensus at Argentina Week: The country is headed in the right direction, beyond disagreements about sequence of policy decisions and speed. 

3️⃣ MERCADOLIBRE ANNOUNCES US$3.4B INVESTMENT FOR 2026 — LOGISTICS, FINTECH, AND 1,900 NEW JOBS
MercadoLibre $MELI used Argentina Week to announce a US$3.4 billion investment plan for 2026, covering logistics infrastructure, e-commerce platform upgrades, and Mercado Pago expansion.
The company said the funds — a mix of capex and opex — will create 1,900 new jobs in Argentina.
CEO @SzAriel: "E-commerce penetration in Argentina is at 15%. We'll reach China's 30% standard — who knows in how many years — and we want to be the protagonists."
📊 By the numbers: MercadoLibre employs 16,700+ people in Argentina. More than 2.7 million SMEs and entrepreneurs sell on the platform. The company exported US$878 million in services last year.

4️⃣ TGS ANNOUNCES US$3B NGL PROJECT — CAPUTO: "THIS IS THE KIND OF INVESTMENT THAT WILL MOVE THE COUNTRY FORWARD"
Transportadora de Gas del Sur (TGS) unveiled a US$3 billion project to industrialize natural gas liquids from Vaca Muerta — the largest infrastructure announcement of Argentina Week.
The 45-month project includes: 
• New gas processing facilities at the Tratayén plant in Neuquén 
• A pipeline from Tratayén to Bahía Blanca 
• A fractionation and storage plant plus an export terminal in Bahía Blanca
Products: ethane (petrochemical feedstock), propane, butane, isobutane, and natural gasoline.
📊 The numbers: US$1.2 billion in projected annual exports, 2.7 million tons/year of NGL output, 4,000 direct jobs, 15,000 indirect jobs. Target markets: regional and Asian buyers (no offtake agreements signed yet).
@LuisCaputoAR: "This is the kind of investment we need. This is what's going to move the country forward."
Marcelo Mindlin (Pampa Energía, TGS shareholder): "This is the first plant of this kind built in Argentina in 25 years." He said the project addresses a bottleneck for Vaca Muerta to reach 1.5 million barrels/day by 2031.
Neuquén Governor @Rolo_Figueroa told @BloombergLineaA the announcement was "very important" and noted productive meetings with US companies including Continental Resources.

5️⃣ MORGAN STANLEY'S SEDANO: "INTEREST IN ARGENTINA IS ENORMOUS" — BULLISH ON BONDS, OVERWEIGHT ON EQUITIES
Fernando Sedano, Morgan Stanley's chief economist for Latin America ex-Brazil, told @BloombergLinea_ the bank remains constructive on Argentina — bullish on sovereign bonds and overweight on equities — even amid global volatility from the Iran conflict.
"Interest from international investors is enormous," Sedano said at the start of Argentina Week. He noted that demand now extends beyond bondholders to equity investors who "want, have, had, and plan to have stakes in companies."
📈 On reserves: Sedano called the BCRA's US$3B+ in purchases before the harvest season "solid" and said it exceeded most investors' expectations. Of that amount, ~US$1B went to Bopreal payments. He expects purchases to accelerate in Q2 as the harvest liquidation kicks in.
⛽ On oil: A 10% price increase would be net positive for Argentina's external accounts, even accounting for higher winter gas imports. The bank estimates Argentina can add ~US$4B/year in net energy exports — potentially more at current prices. The risk: oil price pass-through could add pressure to inflation.
💬 On Macri comparisons: "The questions exist, but they're fewer than last year. When you consider where we came from and where we are today, if you have doubts as an analyst, you have to give the authorities the benefit of the doubt."

6️⃣ CAPUTO AND BAUSILI CLOSE ARGENTINA WEEK — CEPO STAYS, ALTERNATIVE FINANCING ON THE TABLE, NO SPECIFIC RESERVE TARGET
@LuisCaputoAR and BCRA chief @Kicker0024 closed Argentina Week at Bank of America with a panel moderated by JPMorgan's Facundo Gómez Minujín and BofA's Sebastián Loketek.
On the cepo: Bausili said no changes are coming in the short term. He noted there are no longer foreign investor funds trapped in the country, and the remaining restrictions function as "containment mechanisms" to keep working capital in pesos. "If you don't want to participate in the official FX market, you can go to the financial market. It's completely legal."
On reserves: There's no specific accumulation target. "We'll buy reserves to the extent that people demand pesos. We don't want to push the market in either direction." Bausili said the economy has doubled its monetary balances in two years but they remain low by historical and regional standards.
On financing: Caputo said the government is exploring alternatives to international bond markets — including asset sales and local placements — to cover upcoming maturities. He noted the capital payments on Global bonds amount to ~US$1.5B every six months. "I'm not here to fight the market. The solution is to keep paying."
On country risk: Caputo said Argentina's risk premium will converge to where the economic team believes it should be. "In the long run, fundamentals always prevail." He estimated that if country risk fell to 200bp, Argentina could grow 7-8% annually.
Bank of America co-president Jim DeMare opened the event noting: "I don't remember ever being in a room this full."

7️⃣ STURZENEGGER AT COUNCIL OF THE AMERICAS: "IF YOU LET US BE FREE, WE'LL DO INCREDIBLE THINGS"
@fedesturze presented his deregulation agenda to investors at the Council of the Americas, deploying a barrage of data points:
• Rent prices down 30% after repealing the rental law 
• Yerba mate prices cut in half after dissolving the regulatory authority 
• SME exports growing at 40% annualized 
• State payroll reduced by 65,000 since December 2023 • 400,000 jobs created in the first two years 
• 1.2 million Argentines now have commercial flights within 50km of home (vs. zero before deregulation) 
• Satellite internet users went from zero to accelerating coverage in areas that never had connectivity
On sustainability: Sturzenegger identified three "blocking agents" that historically prevented change in Argentina — unions, crony capitalism, and Kirchnerism — calling them the vertices of a "Bermuda Triangle" that trapped the country for decades. He argued that labor reform weakens centralized union bargaining, trade opening erodes crony rents, and the single paper ballot reduced electoral manipulation.
"If you don't undo the legal architecture through which these groups obtained their privileges, you'll never eliminate them. They'll always be there as resistance — like the clergy and landowners in the French Revolution, always trying a counter-revolution."
On patents: Sturzenegger announced Argentina will join the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) this year — "the greatest progress for our scientific and technological world in probably 50 years."

8️⃣ YPF's MARÍN: STOCK SPLIT UNDER CONSIDERATION, US$12B IN EXPORTS BY 2030, ARGENTINA LNG MAY ADD A PARTNER
YPF CEO @HoracioMarin_ok told @BloombergLineaA  the company is analyzing a stock split on the Buenos Aires exchange to improve access for retail investors. "By lowering the price, you give access to people with different income levels. I think it's a good decision."
The split would apply only to the local listing, not the NYSE ADR. Marín said BYMA raised the possibility and YPF is evaluating whether it's feasible. The company also plans to allow stock purchases through its fintech app.
📈 On exports: Marín projected YPF could export US$12 billion annually by 2030-2031 — US$7B from crude (250,000 bpd at ~US$70/barrel) plus US$5B from gas and energy. The step-change depends on the Vaca Muerta Sur pipeline (VMOS), expected to begin operations between December 2026 and January 2027.
🤝 On Argentina LNG: Marín said YPF doesn't need more partners for the project but is in conversations with a potential additional participant. "We'll see if it gets defined quickly — it's possible." Current partners: ENI and XRG (ADNOC's international investment arm). JPMorgan is advising on financing; FID expected between late March and mid-April.
💰 On losses: Marín called recent net losses "transitory" and "one-shots" — driven by conventional field exit costs, deferred tax effects, and an ARCA regularization charge. He said 2026 will be "cleaner" and predicted YPF is on track for "the third-largest result in company history."

9️⃣ VISTA'S GALUCCIO: "WE HAVE A LOT OF ELASTICITY" — HIGHER OIL PRICES COULD ACCELERATE VACA MUERTA CAPEX
Vista Energy $VIST CEO Miguel Galuccio told Bloomberg TV that higher oil prices from the Iran conflict could accelerate investment in Vaca Muerta, where production plans were built around US$65 Brent.
"We have a lot of elasticity. We drill a four-well pad in four months. So if we push the button today because we have more capex due to higher oil prices, in four months we'll be delivering more to the world."
Galuccio said it's too early to know if Vista will revise its 2026 capex guidance of US$1.5-1.6 billion.
📈 The opportunity: Vista shares are up ~27% YTD and trading near all-time highs (~US$62). The VMOS pipeline, expected in early 2027, would add 180,000 bpd of export capacity initially, with room for 700,000+.
"The most important thing is how we recover energy security. Where's the rest of the oil going to come from? I think Argentina has a role as a new player on the global stage."
On US shale migration: Galuccio noted Continental Resources became the first US independent to invest in Argentina last year. "We've been blessed by the rock. But to accelerate, we need more capital, more oilfield service providers, more product suppliers, more competition."

🔟 IDEA CLOSES ARGENTINA WEEK — CEOs FLAG COMPETITIVENESS GAPS, CAPUTO URGES PATIENCE ON SPEED OF CHANGE
The final chapter of Argentina Week took place Thursday at the Argentine consulate, hosted by IDEA. The panel featured CEOs from Grupo Financiero Galicia, Ford Argentina, Grupo Murchison, and Adecoagro — plus closing remarks from @LuisCaputoAR.
🚗 Ford's Martín Galdeano put numbers on the competitiveness gap: exporting a pickup from Argentina still carries 12-15 points of taxes, vs. subsidies in China and minimal levies in Thailand. Of those 12-15 points, 6-7 come from provincial gross income taxes (ingresos brutos) accumulated across the auto parts chain.
🏦 Galicia's Fabián Kon noted credit/GDP rose from 4-5% to 12% under Milei, but said the mining, energy, and infrastructure investments ahead will require a much more developed capital market. "We lived many years without a currency that works as a unit of account, savings vehicle, and means of payment."
🌾 Adecoagro's  $AGRO Mariano Bosch said he stopped coming to New York four or five years ago because investors wouldn't take meetings. "Now you say 'I'm coming, can I see you?' and they say 'sure.' That changed."
📈 Caputo's close: The minister asked attendees to value the speed at which real investment is responding — unusual in stabilization programs, where financial flows typically lead and real economy investment lags by years. "Speed matters, because in the meantime there are people suffering. We have very high poverty levels."
Governor panels covered mining (San Juan, Catamarca, Jujuy, Salta, Santa Cruz) and energy (Neuquén, Río Negro, Mendoza, Chubut, Corrientes). In off-the-record conversations, governors showed alignment with the national government on RIGI but flagged some friction over provincial tax discussions.
